results.title: I asked ChatGPT to ROAST Data Analysts, and it did not disappoint 😂 * Prepare to be completely obliterated* ⬇️ Data analysts are basically the Excel-loving, PowerPoint-wielding middle children of… | Jess Ramos ⚡️ | 292 comments
results.content: of data analysts complaining that parsing complex queries written by AI is more painful than writing them yourself 😵💫 But if you’re trying to democratize data access for your entire organization, LLMs will never work: Realistically complex schemas ✖️ lots of users asking questions every day = recipe for disaster 👎 And it doesn’t matter if you build or buy, it’s a fundamental limitation of the technology itself. But as I can only assume you’re not irresponsible enough to deploy at scale a system that hallucinate 40% of the time, your real cost will be time and energy. 👉 Don’t let the hype fool you, you’ll be better off working on data quality and better data modeling than endlessly tinkering with LLMs. #LLM #hallucinations #Transformers #text2SQL #Analytics #selfserve #AI #BI [...] New ChatGPT features you should try Custom GPTs — Your Personal AI Assistants Think of this as building your own AI intern. You can now create specialized GPTs tailored to your role. Analysts build GPTs for SQL debugging, marketers create GPTs for campaign copy, and founders use GPTs for pitch decks. 👉 Example: built a “Sales GPT” that automatically analyzes CRM data and drafts weekly updates for the team. Why it matters: Instead of generic answers, you get role-specific expertise. Memory That Actually Works In 2025, ChatGPT now remembers context across conversations. That means if you ask it to write SQL queries today, next week it’ll still remember your database schema. Or if you’re writing a blog, it remembers your writing style. Real win: I use memory for repetitive tasks like [...] AI code for developers 7.Copilot in Excel AI assistant that analyzes datasets and builds formulas instantly. These tools don’t replace your expertise, they magnify it. Together, they make research faster, visuals smarter, and data storytelling more intuitive. 3️⃣ Data + AI = Insight, Not Overload Before this program, I saw Power BI mainly as a reporting tool. Now I see it as a bridge between human insight and machine intelligence. Using Power BI alongside AI tools like Copilot, we can now: 1. Clean and analyze data faster with AI-assisted queries 2. Build predictive visuals and dashboards in minutes 3. Turn spreadsheets into interactive stories powered by data In short, AI transforms Power BI from a static reporting tool into a decision engine. My biggest takeaway? AI won’t replace people

results.content:
Introducing: Bruin AI Data Analyst 🚀
We have built an AI Data Analyst that lives in your Slack. You can tag it, ask any data questions, and make it go through all your data warehouse. It can analyze complex datasets, create charts, and export CSV files.
You must have heard quite a lot of AI data analysts by now, but this one is different:
- It builds a semantic understanding of your data warehouse through various methods.
- It allows you to connect your dbt repo, Airflow code, your data catalog, or a spreadsheet that contains some mapping.
- It understands your repos, and it can create pull requests with fixes for your pipelines.
- It learns from your behavior, the data, and your instructions. [...] Tanay Bensu Yurttürk, graphic

Our data pipelines start behaving better after discovering Bruin… Do you think that is a coincidence?

Alberto J Gomez, graphic

results.title: AI Won't Replace Data Analysts, But Here's What Will | Kedeisha Bryan, MBA posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: me lazier about verification." "Now, with LLM-generated content, it’s hard to even build mental models for what might go wrong, because there’s such a long tail of possible errors." (LLM means Large Language Model) Why this matters: "Firstly, if consumers can’t comprehend complex ideas or notice errors, they’re easier to manipulate. This isn’t just about misinformation in the dramatic sense. It’s more mundane than that. If I can’t tell whether a piece of code is actually robust or just looks robust, I might ship something broken." "Second, taste degrades. Taste—in any domain—depends on a feedback loop: you notice when something is good, you notice when something is bad, and over time you develop judgment. But if you stop being able to notice the difference, you stop developing that [...] I found a blog post with well thought-out views on AI by a data analyst. Here are some highlights directly quoted: "AI has overused the tools designed to aid human comprehension to the point where I’ve stopped noticing them. - - When every paragraph has a metaphor, you stop noticing metaphors. - - AI deploys communication tools and rhetorical devices not because the content requires them, but because they pattern-match on what “good writing” or “robust code” looked like in the training data." "Separately, my ability to verify whether something is correct has gotten worse. - - AI generates plausible content almost instantly, but verifying it still requires human effort that hasn’t scaled. - - The ease of regeneration has made me lazier about verification." "Now, with LLM-generated [...] The "AI will take your job" narrative is missing the point. And it's keeping talented career changers from a massive opportunity in tech. I coach career changers into $100K+ data careers, here's what I actually see happening 👇🏽 AI isn't replacing data analysts. It's replacing 𝘵𝘢𝘴𝘬𝘴. The boring ones. The repetitive ones. The ones you didn't want to do anyway. Basic report pulling? AI handles that now. Cleaning simple datasets? Automated. Writing SQL queries from scratch? ChatGPT can help. But here's what AI can't do: It can't sit in a meeting and ask the right questions. It can't look at a dashboard and 𝘬𝘯𝘰𝘸 something feels off. It can't tell a story that makes the CFO change their mind. It can't understand the politics of why a project matters. These are human skills. And they're more

results.title: Will AI Replace Data Analysts? 2026 Reality Check
results.content:
This knowledge lets the analyst spot when data doesn't match reality. If the attribution model says brand campaigns have negative ROI, but the business is growing, the analyst knows the model is broken—not the strategy. AI cannot make that judgment without business context.

The best analysts treat every project as a business problem first and a data problem second. They ask "What decision are we trying to make?" before pulling any data. This focus prevents analysis paralysis and ensures the work drives action.

### Stakeholder management and expectation setting [...] AI makes this model scalable. A single embedded analyst, supported by automated pipelines and conversational analytics, can serve a team of 20–30. They delegate routine queries to AI and focus on strategic questions. The business team gets faster answers and better context.

### AI as the junior analyst

The best teams treat AI tools as junior team members. They're fast, tireless, and handle repetitive tasks without complaint. But they need direction, quality control, and validation.

Senior analysts assign work to AI: "Pull last month's performance by channel." "Run the attribution model with a 30-day lookback." "Flag any campaigns where CPA exceeded threshold." The AI executes; the analyst reviews and interprets. [...] Consider two scenarios: (1) CPM increased because Meta prioritized brand safety after a scandal, shrinking inventory. (2) CPM increased because your creative fatigued and relevance scores tanked. The data looks identical—costs rose 30% in both cases. The correct response is completely different: in scenario one, you wait it out or shift budget; in scenario two, you refresh creative immediately.

AI struggles with ambiguous and messy data including missing values, requiring human validation for data quality. An analyst with context recognizes that last week's LinkedIn campaign paused mid-flight because the sales team closed the target account early—not because performance degraded. AI interprets the drop as a failure signal.

### Stakeholder communication and narrative building
